scope_note: Hand-curated cluster-analysis layer over the CIM Library corpus. Metadata is used to seed candidate neighborhoods, but public cluster claims should be argued from paper notes, papers, and official artifacts; they are not rankings, coverage scores, or affiliation graphs.
methodology: Use existing paper metadata only as a seed for candidate neighborhoods: Axis A/B placement, technologies, workloads, first-class objects, rewrite objects, artifact status, author/group fields, and public note summaries.
Before strengthening a cluster, inspect the actual corpus notes, paper claims, and official artifacts for the stack object, transformation path, backend handoff, and boundary of the claim.
Write cluster content as analysis paragraphs or prose graphs, not as prose descriptions of metadata clustering.
Prefer coarse families with repeated stack objects or visible tool lineage over fine-grained author-network claims, and keep narrow clusters narrow when the binding object changes.
Mark uncertain boundaries directly and link back to scoped atlas views for inspection.

clusters.label: Crossbar DNN compiler and simulator toolchain line
clusters.summary: A set of NVM/RRAM crossbar PIM stacks where ONNX-style DNN graphs, array groups, PIM functional units, pseudo-instructions, and simulator configurations become the reusable compiler/backend boundary.
clusters.binding_theme: Graph-to-array-group mapping plus instruction/configuration artifacts for crossbar PIM DNN inference.
object_flow: Entry object: a DNN model graph, usually ONNX, JSON, or an equivalent graph representation.
Binding/rewrite: layers and weights are placed onto crossbar arrays, array groups, PIMFUs, cores, and chip-level configuration state.
Backend handoff: the durable object is an instruction stream, pseudo-instruction set, hardware configuration, or simulator-facing execution artifact.
Boundary: the toolchain continuity is visible, but paper-by-paper schema compatibility still needs artifact-level checking.
shared_rewrite_objects: numeric format, precision, quantization, or approximation
evidence: PIMCOMP, PIMSIM-NN, PIMSYN/PIMSYN-NN, PIMACC, and PIM EDA entries all expose crossbar/PIM resources and compiler or simulator boundaries rather than only circuit-level macro data.
The corpus notes repeatedly name array groups, PIMFUs, per-core instructions, hardware configuration files, or simulator-facing instruction/config artifacts.

The author/group fields and artifacts indicate a visible Xiaoming Chen / Yinhe Han related toolchain family across several entries, but the cluster label is based on stack artifacts rather than an affiliation map.
clusters.uncertainty: Treat this as a toolchain-line cluster. It should not be read as a complete institutional or collaboration graph, and PIM EDA is repository-centered rather than a single paper entry.

working_groups.label: Xiaoming Chen / Yinhe Han PIM toolchain family
working_groups.evidence: The entries list overlapping authors/groups and public repositories around PIMCOMP-NN, PIMSIM-NN, PIMSYN-NN, and PIM-Toolchain.
working_groups.uncertainty: Coarse family label only; no claim about all contributors, affiliations, or internal project structure.

working_group_investigations.label: Xiaoming Chen / Yinhe Han PIM compiler-simulator toolchain
working_group_investigations.scope: Treat as a toolchain lineage around crossbar-PIM DNN compilation, synthesis, simulation, and verification artifacts, not as a department-level affiliation or full author network.
working_group_investigations.why_coherent: The shared object is a deployable crossbar-DNN stack boundary: ONNX or JSON model input, crossbar/core/chip configuration, weight replication and pipeline choices, instruction or pseudo-instruction outputs, verification files, and simulator-facing artifacts.
continuity_evidence: PIMCOMP-NN documents ONNX-to-JSON conversion, backend compilation from JSON models, weight-replication strategies, pipeline granularity choices, and optional saved verification, intermediate, and simulation outputs.
PIMSIM-NN and PIMACC entries reuse the same broad backend vocabulary: array groups, core mapping, hardware configs, instruction streams, DAC/ADC parameters, and simulator evaluation.
The corpus also has a repository-centered PIM EDA entry for the same broad toolchain area, but it should remain supporting evidence because it aggregates artifacts rather than representing one paper.
working_group_investigations.boundary: Do not merge all crossbar or RRAM papers into this group. The group is about visible compiler/simulator artifact continuity, not merely shared technology.
next_checks: Check whether PIMSIM-NN and PIMSYN-NN artifact READMEs define file formats compatible with PIMCOMP-NN outputs.
Separate paper-specific compiler objects such as PIMCOMP array groups from broader repository-suite objects if this becomes a detail page.

clusters.label: Digital SRAM-CIM macro generation and precision specialization
clusters.summary: A lower-stack family centered on DCIM macro templates, precision fields, subcircuit choices, BIST or EDA flows, and physical or macro-level backend artifacts rather than whole-model compiler IR.
clusters.binding_theme: Config-as-IR for macro templates, numeric formats, and backend generation constraints.
object_flow: Entry object: a macro specification names rows, columns, precision, cells, subcircuits, and peripheral choices before workload mapping dominates the stack.
Binding/rewrite: generation or search specializes the macro through template selection, numeric format choices, BIST, approximate arithmetic, or EDA constraints.
Backend handoff: the durable object is RTL, SPICE, DEF, GDSII, layout, or a validated macro-level report rather than a full model compiler dialect.
Boundary: the cluster is about lower-stack macro materialization; OpenACM-style approximate search is related but not the same lineage.
shared_rewrite_objects: numeric format, precision, quantization, or approximation
fault, variation, calibration, or accuracy handling
evidence: AutoDCIM, ARCTIC, SynDCIM, OpenC2, and OpenACMv2 are all classified around SRAM/digital-CIM macro or approximate-DCIM generation and optimization.
Their frontmatter foregrounds macro topology, numeric precision, sub-array or cell-level structures, BIST/test flow, EDA flow, or approximate hardware choices.
The entries repeatedly place the work in Axis A1 or A1-adjacent roles with B1/B4/B6 middle-layer styles.
clusters.uncertainty: The cluster intentionally groups by stack object and backend surface. It does not assert that every entry shares one project lineage, and some supporting entries are broader co-optimization frameworks.

working_groups.label: HKUST / digital-CIM macro compiler line
working_groups.relationship: repeated macro-compiler and precision-oriented DCIM entries
working_groups.evidence: The current author/group fields mention HKUST or overlapping Cheng/Tsui digital-CIM macro compiler contributors across several entries.
working_groups.uncertainty: Tentative coarse label; use the paper pages for exact author lists and institutional context.

working_groups.label: OpenACM / approximate-DCIM optimization thread
working_groups.relationship: accuracy-constrained approximate arithmetic and macro co-optimization
working_groups.evidence: OpenACMv2 makes approximate compressor choices, SRAM macro parameters, PEA-GNN prediction, accuracy budgets, and circuit-level sizing explicit optimization objects.
working_groups.uncertainty: Single-entry thread in the current corpus. It is supporting evidence for this cluster, not part of the core HKUST macro compiler lineage.

working_group_investigations.label: HKUST / digital-CIM macro compiler line
working_group_investigations.scope: Treat as a DCIM macro-generation and physical-backend line centered on spec/config-to-RTL/netlist/layout flows, not as a DNN workload compiler lineage.
working_group_investigations.why_coherent: The common object is the macro/backend configuration: rows and columns, input and weight precision, SRAM bitcell and compute-cell choices, multiplier, mux, adder and accumulator structures, subcircuit libraries, BIST/test modes, and EDA layout artifacts.
continuity_evidence: AutoDCIM is positioned in the notes as a spec-to-layout DCIM macro compiler with array partitioning and template-based generation.
OpenC2 exposes the public macro-generator side of the line: top-level macro parameters and technology/library inputs generate Verilog, SPICE, DEF, and GDSII artifacts.

ARCTIC and SynDCIM deepen the precision, subcircuit, and BIST side of the same lower-stack question, while OpenACMv2 is related as an accuracy-constrained approximate-DCIM optimization flow rather than the same lineage.
working_group_investigations.boundary: Keep application-level CIM compilers out of this group unless their first-class output is the macro/backend flow. Keep OpenACMv2 as supporting evidence when discussing approximate arithmetic DSE rather than as a core HKUST lineage claim.
next_checks: Inspect OpenC2 output examples and generator scripts before writing a detail page about generated artifact formats.
Check whether ARCTIC and SynDCIM expose reusable spec fields beyond the paper descriptions; current evidence is mostly paper-level.

working_group_investigations.label: OpenACM / approximate-DCIM optimization boundary
working_group_investigations.scope: Treat as a single-artifact approximate-DCIM DSE thread where approximate arithmetic, macro configuration, and transistor-level validity become optimization state. Do not merge it into the core spec-to-layout macro-generator lineage.
working_group_investigations.why_coherent: The common object is accuracy-constrained hardware search state: approximate compressor type, compressor slot, partial-product column approximation region, bit significance, SRAM rows/columns/mux/array count, transistor sizing vector, PVT or Monte Carlo condition, and MRED/NMED accuracy budget.
continuity_evidence: OpenACMv2's Level-I optimization searches multiplier compressor assignments, lower-significance partial-product approximation regions, and SRAM macro configurations under error and PPA objectives.

PEA-GNN turns a multiplier compressor assignment into a graph for predicting MRED, NMED, delay, area, and power, making approximate arithmetic structure a model-facing object rather than only a generated netlist.
Level-II refines selected compressor cells and SRAM bitcells with SPICE/PVT/Monte Carlo-style sizing while preserving the selected approximate truth-table semantics.
working_group_investigations.boundary: Keep OpenACMv2 as supporting evidence for the digital macro cluster because it shares SRAM/DCIM backend objects, but distinguish it from AutoDCIM/OpenC2/SynDCIM-style macro materialization flows. Its central object is an accuracy/PPA search trajectory, not a workload compiler or full-stack IR.
next_checks: Inspect the OpenACM repository's documented optimization outputs before claiming stable generated-file schemas.
Compare OpenACMv2's compressor-assignment state with SynDCIM's subcircuit synthesis state to see whether both can share one detail-page table.
Check whether paper-level image/CIFAR-10 accuracy budgets have machine-readable configs in the artifact.

clusters.label: Analog CIM modeling, nonideality, and calibrated cost baselines
clusters.summary: A simulator and modeling cluster where the first-class objects are device/circuit/array hierarchies, ADC/sensing precision, operand distributions, nonidealities, calibration state, and mapping-aware cost estimates.
clusters.binding_theme: Backend contracts and cost models for analog or hybrid CIM evaluation.
object_flow: Entry object: a model, workload, or mapping problem is projected onto device, circuit, array, tile, chip, or Timeloop-style hierarchy descriptions.
Binding/rewrite: the modeling layer makes ADC/DAC precision, sensing, bit slicing, operand distributions, nonidealities, and calibration reusable state.
Backend handoff: the durable object is a cost, accuracy, mapping, or calibration report that feeds architecture search or backend comparison.
Boundary: the cluster is methodological; NeuroSim, CiMLoop, GENIEx, and NAX share modeling objects, not one compiler stack.
shared_rewrite_objects: numeric format, precision, quantization, or approximation
fault, variation, calibration, or accuracy handling
evidence: NeuroSIM and CiMLoop are explicitly classified as simulator/cost-model entries with reusable hardware-resource and configuration boundaries.
GENIEx and NAX add accuracy, variation, and co-design concerns around memristive crossbar inference.
The shared vocabulary is not a single compiler IR; it is a repeated backend model surface naming array hierarchy, peripheral precision, numeric format, and nonideality state.
clusters.uncertainty: This is a methodological cluster, not one working group. The entries come from several research lines and should remain separated when discussing authorship.

working_groups.label: NeuroSim-centered modeling family
working_groups.evidence: The notes identify NeuroSIM / DNN+NeuroSim and NS-Cache as NeuroSim-family artifacts or extensions.
working_groups.uncertainty: Limited to visible artifact and note evidence.

working_groups.label: Timeloop/Accelergy CIM modeling extension
working_groups.evidence: CiMLoop is described as a Timeloop+Accelergy based CIM modeling extension with public MIT artifact metadata.
working_groups.uncertainty: Single-entry group in the current corpus, listed because it is a strong modeling baseline rather than a broad author family.

working_groups.label: GENIEx/NAX nonideality-aware co-design thread
working_groups.relationship: learned nonideality model feeding crossbar-aware search
working_groups.evidence: GENIEx provides a learned analog crossbar nonideality model and PyTorch functional simulator; NAX uses GENIEx-style nonideality feedback in a hardware-aware neural architecture and crossbar-size search loop.
working_groups.uncertainty: Method-continuity label only. GENIEx is a simulator/modeling paper and NAX is a NAS/DSE paper, so keep exact claims tied to their paper notes.

working_group_investigations.label: NeuroSim-centered CIM modeling family
working_group_investigations.scope: Treat as a backend modeling family around device/circuit/array/chip cost and accuracy reports, with DNN wrappers as a bounded frontend path.
working_group_investigations.why_coherent: The common object is a simulator contract: hardware hierarchy, subarray dimensions, memory-cell/device parameters, ADC/sensing precision, buffers/interconnect, nonideality settings, traces, and CSV/report outputs.
continuity_evidence: NeuroSIM / DNN+NeuroSim combines a C++ device/circuit/architecture model with DNN wrappers that map topology, traces, precision, and nonidealities onto chip, tile, processing-element, and synaptic-array hierarchy.

NS-Cache extends the NeuroSim-adjacent modeling style to cache-memory and backend exploration, exposing bank, subarray, mat hierarchy, access mode, refresh timing, interconnect, and Gem5/Ruby bridge parameters.
GENIEx and NAX are related methodologically through analog crossbar nonideality and accuracy-cost feedback, but they should remain separate from a strict NeuroSim artifact lineage.
working_group_investigations.boundary: This is a modeling lineage and methodology cluster, not one unified compiler stack. Keep CiMLoop separate as a Timeloop/Accelergy extension unless discussing shared cost-model abstractions.
next_checks: Inspect NeuroSim and NS-Cache config/report formats before claiming a stable shared schema.
Compare CiMLoop's YAML/Timeloop boundary with NeuroSim's C++/trace/report boundary as a separate modeling-method contrast.

working_group_investigations.label: CiMLoop as a Timeloop/Accelergy CIM modeling extension
working_group_investigations.scope: Treat as a single-artifact modeling baseline around Timeloop+Accelergy extensions for CIM, not as a broad working-group lineage.
working_group_investigations.why_coherent: The common object is a config-and-modeling contract: YAML/Jinja architecture hierarchy, component definitions, workload/problem dimensions, mapper settings, reuse/coalescing directives, encoding and slicing functions, operand distributions, and Accelergy plug-in component models.
continuity_evidence: The CiMLoop note identifies the reusable boundary as Timeloop-style YAML/Jinja configs plus Python helper functions for architecture hierarchy, components, variables, workload, mapper settings, and encoding/slicing.

CiMLoop exposes CIM-specific modeling objects that are not just cost-table entries: memory cells, CIM units, row and column drivers, ADCs, DACs, shift-add units, bit slicing, tensor-specific reuse, coalescing, bypass, operand distributions, and calibration scales.

The paper-note evidence positions CiMLoop as adjacent to NeuroSim, MNSIM, and fixed accelerator simulators, but its boundary is different: Timeloop mappings and Accelergy component actions rather than a C++ device/circuit simulator or PyTorch nonideality wrapper.
working_group_investigations.boundary: Keep this separate from the NeuroSim-centered lineage. CiMLoop belongs in the same analog modeling cluster because it names overlapping hardware and nonideality objects, but its artifact contract is a Timeloop/Accelergy configuration and mapping-search surface.
next_checks: Inspect representative CiMLoop YAML/Jinja examples before claiming stable field names beyond the current paper note.
Compare CiMLoop's per-component reuse/coalescing directives with NeuroSim/GENIEx modeling boundaries to identify which abstractions could become a cross-tool comparison table.
Check whether CiMLoop reproduction notebooks expose generated result schemas suitable for future detail-page evidence.

working_group_investigations.label: GENIEx/NAX nonideality-to-search thread
working_group_investigations.scope: Treat as a methodological thread where analog crossbar nonideality becomes a simulator-visible feedback signal for model or hardware search. This is not a full compiler stack and not a broad author-network claim.
working_group_investigations.why_coherent: The common object is nonideal MVM behavior made reusable enough to affect higher-level decisions: voltage vectors, conductance matrices, crossbar size, bit streams and slices, ADC precision, shift-add reconstruction, per-layer operation-to-crossbar binding, and accuracy/cost feedback.
continuity_evidence: GENIEx models data-dependent memristive-crossbar nonidealities as a learned current-ratio surrogate over input-voltage and conductance tensors, then injects that behavior into PyTorch convolution and linear MVM layers.

GENIEx's simulator path names the relevant backend objects for accuracy studies: tiled crossbar MVM, positive/negative weight paths, bit slicing, bit streaming, ADC quantization, accumulator width, and shift-add reconstruction.

NAX takes the next step at the search layer by coupling CNN operation choices with crossbar-size choices, using hardware-efficiency lookup tables and GENIEx-style nonideality-aware inference during architecture-parameter updates.
working_group_investigations.boundary: Keep this narrower than the analog modeling cluster as a whole. It does not include CiMLoop's Timeloop/Accelergy config surface or NeuroSim's device/circuit/architecture reporting lineage, and NAX lacks a public artifact in the checked corpus.
next_checks: Inspect the GENIEx functional-model repository before claiming stable simulator APIs or model-weight availability.
Check whether NAX exposes any supplementary search tables or configs; current evidence is paper-level only.
Compare GENIEx/NAX against NeuroSim and CiMLoop in a future detail table by asking which object is mutable: nonideality surrogate, mapping config, or architecture/search candidate.

clusters.label: UPMEM real-hardware runtime and benchmark layer
clusters.summary: A real-system PIM cluster where the central objects are DPUs, MRAM/WRAM state, host-device batches, runtime array metadata, tasklets, graph partitions, and benchmark harnesses.
clusters.binding_theme: Runtime-state and memory-layout abstractions for commercial UPMEM DRAM-PIM execution.
object_flow: Entry object: a benchmark, array program, graph kernel, or optimization workload is divided between host code and UPMEM DPU execution.
Binding/rewrite: runtime state names DPU sets, tasklets, MRAM/WRAM placement, host-DPU transfers, partitions, collectives, and merge points.
Backend handoff: the durable object is runnable host code, DPU kernels, Makefile or CLI parameters, datasets, and profiling output.
Boundary: the binding object is real-hardware execution state, not an explicit compiler IR or simulator-only API.
shared_rewrite_objects: runtime scheduling, placement, batching, or data movement
evidence: PrIM, SimplePIM, PIM-Opt, and PIM-TC all target UPMEM or real DRAM-PIM execution rather than analog crossbar simulation.
Their notes foreground DPU resources, host/DPU transfer state, MRAM/WRAM placement, tasklets, array or graph partitions, and measured real-system workloads.
The cluster is primarily Axis A6 and B7/B4, making runtime state the reusable object family.
clusters.uncertainty: The working-group label should remain broad. The entries involve recurring SAFARI/Mutlu-linked real-PIM work, but individual papers have distinct coauthor sets and application domains.

working_groups.label: SAFARI / real-PIM benchmarking and runtime family
working_groups.relationship: recurring UPMEM benchmark, runtime, and workload studies
working_groups.evidence: The author/group fields and artifacts repeatedly reference CMU-SAFARI, Onur Mutlu, or related real-PIM repositories across the UPMEM entries.
working_groups.uncertainty: Coarse visible-publication family only; not a full author or affiliation map.

working_group_investigations.label: SAFARI / UPMEM real-hardware runtime and benchmark line
working_group_investigations.scope: Treat as a real-system UPMEM runtime/benchmark family where the reusable object is host-DPU execution state, not an explicit compiler dialect.
working_group_investigations.why_coherent: The common object is a host/DPU runtime boundary: DPU sets, tasklets, MRAM/WRAM staging, CPU-DPU transfers, per-DPU partitions, benchmark inputs, host merges, collectives, and timing/energy scripts.
continuity_evidence: PrIM establishes the benchmark and characterization baseline with host code, DPU kernels, Makefile parameters, datasets, and profiling outputs.
SimplePIM raises the abstraction to PIM-resident arrays, array IDs, collectives, map/reduce/zip iterators, and management-table state.
PIM-Opt and PIM-TC show workload-specific deepening of the same backend boundary for distributed optimization and graph triangle counting.
working_group_investigations.boundary: Do not treat this as an author-network map. The group label is about repeated real-UPMEM backend objects and public artifacts, and individual papers remain distinct in workload and algorithm scope.
next_checks: Compare artifact CLI and Makefile knobs across PrIM, SimplePIM, PIM-Opt, and PIM-TC to see whether a shared UPMEM backend contract can be summarized.
Separate general runtime abstractions from workload-specific mapping objects such as color triplets or ADMM synchronization if this becomes a detail page.

clusters.label: PIMeval/PIMsynth DRAM-PIM API and bit-serial compiler line
clusters.summary: A narrow digital DRAM-PIM cluster in which PIM data-object APIs, benchmark kernels, simulator commands, and bit-serial operation DAGs define the backend-facing programming boundary.
clusters.binding_theme: PIM API objects and bit-serial instruction generation for digital or mixed DRAM-PIM targets, with PIMeval acting as a benchmark/simulator boundary and PIMsynth compiling into that boundary.
object_flow: Entry object: a benchmark kernel, PIM data object, or Verilog/GenLib operation enters as a PIM API object or bit-serial logic graph.
Binding/rewrite: compilation lowers the operation through IR, scheduling, assembly, test generation, and microprogram-like command sequences.
Backend handoff: the durable object targets PIMeval-style simulation, command statistics, and validation harnesses.
Boundary: the cluster stays narrow; HARMONI is adjacent but centered on hierarchy mapping rather than PIMeval API command compilation.
evidence: PIMeval/PIMbench exposes a public C++ PIM API, PIM object IDs, hardware configs, command statistics, and benchmark kernels for modeled digital DRAM-PIM targets.
PIMsynth exposes a public compiler path from Verilog and GenLib-defined bit-serial PIM operations through graph/IR stages to PIMeval-backed microprogram validation.
The entries are distinct from UPMEM runtime studies because they center on simulator/API/compiler boundaries, not commercial host-DPU execution.
clusters.uncertainty: Medium-high confidence for the narrow PIMeval/PIMsynth line. Keep the cluster small until additional papers expose the same API/compiler boundary; do not fold in graph-only LLM mapping simulators just because they share DRAM-PIM or LavaLab labels.

working_groups.label: UVA LavaLab PIM API and compiler artifacts
working_groups.evidence: The entries list UVA LavaLab repositories for PIMeval/PIMbench and PIMsynth, and the notes describe PIMsynth code generation through PIMeval APIs.
working_groups.uncertainty: Coarse artifact-family label only. HARMONI remains related through LavaLab and DRAM-PIM modeling, but its central object is an LLM task graph plus memory hierarchy rather than the PIM API/bit-serial compiler boundary.

working_group_investigations.label: UVA LavaLab PIM API to bit-serial compiler line
working_group_investigations.scope: Treat as a narrow public-artifact line joining a PIM API/benchmark simulator boundary to a bit-serial compiler that targets that boundary.
working_group_investigations.why_coherent: The common object is the simulator-facing PIM command/program boundary: PIMeval names PIM objects, device configs, API commands, micro-ops, and statistics, while PIMsynth lowers Verilog and GenLib-defined bit-serial operations through IR stages to PIMeval simulation.
continuity_evidence: PIMeval-PIMbench describes libpimeval as the simulation framework and PIMbench as the benchmark suite, with architecture-independent APIs, low-level micro-ops, performance/energy modeling, and detailed statistics.

PIMsynth explicitly lists PIMeval-PIMbench as a third-party dependency and documents a flow from Verilog through BLIF, GenLib, IR-1, LLVM scheduling, IR-2 assembly, a bit-serial microprogram, test generation, and PIMeval simulation.
The contact and citation metadata overlap around UVA LavaLab/Skadron/Venkat-linked artifacts, but the cluster claim rests on the tool boundary rather than authorship.
working_group_investigations.boundary: HARMONI should stay adjacent but outside this narrow group: it uses DRAM-PIM modeling and LavaLab infrastructure, but its central object is a Transformer DFG and tensor-placement hierarchy, not PIMeval API command compilation.
next_checks: Inspect PIMsynth generated .hpp or test output examples to determine how much PIMeval command provenance survives after code generation.
Check PIMeval wiki/API pages for stable command names before promoting this from cluster note to a detail page.

clusters.label: LLM-oriented PIM memory-hierarchy mapping and runtime-state modeling
clusters.summary: A broad LLM/Transformer PIM cluster where the binding object is not a single ISA or API, but model-phase metadata, tensor placement, KV/runtime state, hierarchy binding, and host/PIM or near-memory data movement.
clusters.binding_theme: Mapping Transformer/LLM execution onto memory hierarchies by naming tensor locations, phases, hierarchy resources, runtime state, and communication or batching decisions.
object_flow: Entry object: Transformer or LLM execution is decomposed into phase, layer, tensor, request, token, neuron, or KV/cache state.
Binding/rewrite: mapping binds those objects onto DRAM, NDP units, PIM logic, GPU/NDP placement, wafer/core coordinates, or SRAM-CIM structures.
Backend handoff: the durable object is a placement, movement, batching, synchronization, or remapping plan with latency, energy, or serving-behavior reports.
Boundary: this is a comparison set around runtime and hierarchy state, not one artifact lineage.
shared_rewrite_objects: runtime scheduling, placement, batching, or data movement
evidence: HARMONI exposes a public DFG, HarmoniTensor placement metadata, memory-system hierarchy, logic-unit binding, communication edges, sync/aggregation nodes, and LLM model presets.
PAPI and Hermes foreground LLM decoding or GPU/NDP-DIMM execution state, where runtime placement, batching, and memory movement are the operative middle objects.

UniNDP, Ouroboros, and CIM-MXU are included as supporting papers because their metadata links LLM or Transformer workloads to near-DRAM/SRAM-CIM mapping, simulation, or accelerator co-design surfaces.
clusters.uncertainty: Medium confidence because this is a workload-and-state cluster rather than one artifact lineage. HARMONI is the strongest public modeling artifact; several supporting entries are architecture proposals or have no public artifact.

working_groups.label: HARMONI / UVA LavaLab hierarchy-modeling artifact
working_groups.relationship: public LLM memory-hierarchy mapping and cost-model artifact
working_groups.evidence: The HARMONI note identifies a public UVA LavaLab repository with DFG construction, tensor allocation, task mapping, DRAM/model/network configs, performance models, traces, and AE scripts.
working_groups.uncertainty: Single-entry group in the current corpus. It should be compared with, not merged into, the PIMeval/PIMsynth API/compiler line.

working_groups.label: LLM serving and near-memory comparison set
working_groups.relationship: coarse workload-family comparison, not a collaboration claim
working_groups.evidence: The entries share LLM/Transformer workloads and runtime or hierarchy mapping concerns, but differ in technology, artifact status, and backend visibility.
working_groups.uncertainty: Comparison set only; do not read as a research-group lineage.

working_group_investigations.label: HARMONI as a hierarchy-modeling anchor
working_group_investigations.scope: Treat HARMONI as the strongest public artifact anchor for this cluster because it exposes the internal modeling stack. Use the surrounding LLM papers as comparison cases for similar runtime-state and memory-hierarchy questions.
working_group_investigations.why_coherent: The common object is not an ISA. It is a placement-aware execution model: Transformer phase/layer/head metadata, tensor locations, weight/KV rank placement, logic-unit support tables, communication edges, sync/aggregation nodes, and latency/energy traces.
continuity_evidence: The HARMONI repository describes a main CLI, simulation driver, core modeling modules for DFG/mapping/memory/tensor/model allocation, performance estimators, trace generators, report dumpers, visualization, configs, parameter presets, and cached graph/task-mapping artifacts.

The HARMONI note identifies tensor-location metadata and task-to-logic-unit mapping as the core reusable IR lesson: mapping can rewrite the graph by creating subtasks, sync nodes, and aggregation nodes.

PAPI, Hermes, UniNDP, Ouroboros, and CIM-MXU share LLM or Transformer workloads and runtime/hierarchy mapping concerns, but they should be compared case-by-case because their public artifact depth and backend boundaries differ.
working_group_investigations.boundary: Keep this as a workload/state cluster, not a collaboration group. Do not collapse it with the PIMeval/PIMsynth group merely because both involve UVA LavaLab or DRAM-PIM modeling.
next_checks: Check official artifacts or papers for PAPI, Hermes, UniNDP, Ouroboros, and CIM-MXU before writing stronger group claims.
Decide whether HARMONI deserves a future detail section on graph rewrite objects: DFG nodes, HarmoniTensor metadata, logic-unit mapping, sync/aggregation insertion, and traces.

working_group_investigations.label: LLM serving and near-memory runtime-state comparison set
working_group_investigations.scope: Treat as a coarse comparison set for LLM-oriented PIM/NDP papers where runtime state, tensor or neuron placement, KV/cache behavior, and memory-hierarchy binding are the common objects. Do not treat it as one research lineage.
working_group_investigations.why_coherent: The common object is a serving-time or model-phase mapping state: request/token parallelism, hot/cold neuron placement, DRAM/NDP hierarchy assignment, wafer/core coordinates, KV page tables, and device-specific scheduling decisions.
continuity_evidence: PAPI makes RLP, TLP, FC-vs-attention kernel class, FC-PIM, Attn-PIM, and PU assignment the scheduler-facing state for heterogeneous HBM/DRAM-PIM decoding.
Hermes makes per-neuron hot/cold status, neuron state and correlation tables, GPU-vs-NDP-DIMM placement, and windowed DIMM remapping the visible runtime object.

UniNDP and Ouroboros provide two contrasting backend surfaces: UniNDP exposes DRAM hierarchy configs, partition/mapping tuples, NDP instructions, and simulator issue state, while Ouroboros exposes wafer/core mapping, token pipeline stages, KV page tables, bitmaps, and fault-remap state.
working_group_investigations.boundary: This is a workload/runtime-state comparison set. HARMONI remains the strongest public graph-and-hierarchy modeling anchor; PAPI, Hermes, and Ouroboros are largely paper-level or partial-artifact evidence; UniNDP is a DRAM-NDP mapping/simulation tool rather than specifically an LLM-serving runtime.
next_checks: Check whether PAPI, Hermes, Ouroboros, and CIM-MXU expose reproducible simulator configs or traces beyond paper-level descriptions.
Separate LLM serving runtime-state objects from general DRAM-NDP mapping objects before promoting this into a detail page.
Compare HARMONI's tensor-placement graph with Hermes/PAPI/Ouroboros runtime-state tables to avoid conflating static model mapping with serving-time scheduling.

clusters.label: Full-stack IR, ISA, and serialized backend boundaries
clusters.summary: A cross-cutting cluster for public or near-public stacks that make the compiler/backend boundary visible through graph IRs, MLIR/DSL fragments, instruction streams, JSON/YAML contracts, ISA-like commands, or chip/emulator addresses.
clusters.binding_theme: The stack object becomes explicit enough to inspect as a serialized compiler, simulator, or chip-facing contract.
object_flow: Entry object: a source graph, mapped layer set, model object, or CIM-IR enters a compiler path that keeps intermediate state inspectable.
Binding/rewrite: lowering binds operators to hardware hierarchy, addresses, instruction fields, calculation parameters, or simulator/chip targets.
Backend handoff: the durable object is serialized as JSON, YAML, assembly, meta-operators, generated scripts, or simulator inputs.
Boundary: the cluster is an inspection path across stacks; shared visibility does not imply shared schema, compatibility, or lineage.
shared_rewrite_objects: runtime scheduling, placement, batching, or data movement
evidence: CIMFlow, PUMA, and OpenCIMTC expose graph-to-backend or chip/emulator-facing boundaries more explicitly than macro-only or benchmark-only entries.
The corpus notes name serialized objects such as CG JSON, hardware/config JSON, per-core ISA JSON, PUMA instruction/compiler artifacts, OpenCIMTC YAML-style CIM-IR, or chip/emulator address state.
This cluster intentionally crosses research groups because the binding theme is representational visibility.
clusters.uncertainty: Medium confidence because the grouped systems differ substantially in technology, maturity, and artifact scope. Use this as an inspection path, not a single research lineage.

working_groups.label: Serialized CIM-IR / compiler-contract examples
working_groups.relationship: comparison set for explicit serialized backend contracts
working_groups.evidence: CIMFlow documents an ONNX-to-CG-to-OP-to-ISA/simulator flow with intermediate compiler outputs, while OpenCIMTC exposes YAML CIM-IR and generated training, optimization, and simulator scripts.
working_groups.uncertainty: Comparison set only. CIMFlow is a digital-CIM compiler/simulator framework and OpenCIMTC is a memristor/RRAM full-stack co-design flow; no lineage or schema compatibility is implied.

working_groups.label: Graph-to-assembly / meta-operator compiler examples
working_groups.relationship: comparison set for backend-visible compiler state
working_groups.evidence: PUMA exposes compiler source, programming-interface headers, tests, generated assembly, and simulator execution, while CIM-MLC exposes codegen, configuration, optimization, and output folders for multi-level meta-operator flows.
working_groups.uncertainty: Comparison set only. PUMA is an architecture-specific archived stack; CIM-MLC is a beta multi-template compiler artifact.

working_groups.label: Public full-stack artifact examples
working_groups.evidence: Each representative paper has public artifact or documentation evidence in the corpus and exposes backend-facing compiler objects.
working_groups.uncertainty: Comparison set only; no shared authorship implied.

working_group_investigations.label: CIMFlow/OpenCIMTC serialized compiler-contract comparison
working_group_investigations.scope: Treat as a comparison of explicit serialized compiler contracts: CIMFlow's digital-CIM compiler/simulator files and OpenCIMTC's memristor CIM-IR plus generated scripts. This is not a lineage claim.
working_group_investigations.why_coherent: The common object is a compiler state that survives outside the compiler process: source graph, hardware/config parameters, mapped layer or graph state, backend calculation parameters, generated instruction or simulator code, and run outputs.
continuity_evidence: CIMFlow documentation describes a two-level compiler from ONNX through CG-level graph partitioning and OP-level ISA generation, with separate commands for CG compilation, OP compilation, and cycle-accurate simulation.

CIMFlow's CLI examples make the file boundary concrete: CG compilation can emit an `instructions.json`-style intermediate, OP compilation consumes CG output and emits executable instructions, and simulation consumes compiled instructions.

OpenCIMTC's README workflow makes the YAML/script boundary concrete: ONNX is converted to mapped CIM-IR YAML, then the flow generates PDT training code, optimized IR YAML, simulator code, inference code, and weight files.
working_group_investigations.boundary: Do not describe these as compatible IRs. CIMFlow's contract is organized around digital-CIM CG/OP/ISA/simulator stages, while OpenCIMTC's contract is organized around Attribute/Mapping/Calculation YAML plus hardware-aware training, optimization, and simulator or chip execution.
next_checks: Inspect representative generated CIMFlow `instructions.json` and OpenCIMTC `*_mapped_ir*.yaml` files before making a field-level comparison table.
Check whether either project exposes a schema or verifier beyond examples and Python classes.
Keep analog/memristor calculation knobs separate from digital-CIM ISA fields when comparing backend contracts.

working_group_investigations.label: PUMA/CIM-MLC hidden graph-to-backend state comparison
working_group_investigations.scope: Treat as a comparison of compiler stacks where the most reusable state is partly hidden in graph objects, pass order, placement state, and codegen templates, then becomes visible as PUMA assembly or CIM-MLC meta-operator outputs.
working_group_investigations.why_coherent: The common object is graph-to-backend lowering state: model graph nodes, hardware hierarchy, tensor or MVM partitioning, resource placement, communication or pipeline decisions, register/address state, and generated instruction or meta-operator streams.
continuity_evidence: The PUMA compiler repository exposes `include`, `src`, and `test` directories, matching the paper-note reading that the public boundary is a programming interface plus examples that generate PUMA-specific assembly and simulator inputs.

The PUMA note identifies the hidden state as compiler-built model graph, tensor tiling, MVMU/core/tile placement, FIFO and shared-memory allocation, MVM coalescing, register allocation, and simulator configuration.

The CIM-MLC repository exposes `codegen`, `configs`, `optimization`, `output`, and `simulator` folders, matching the note's reading that ONNX annotations, architecture Python configs, pass-local state, and tier-specific codegen templates are the effective compiler state before meta-operator output.
working_group_investigations.boundary: Do not merge PUMA and CIM-MLC as one artifact lineage. PUMA is a narrow ISA-programmable memristor accelerator stack with archived compiler code; CIM-MLC is a multi-level scheduling framework over selected CIM templates with beta artifact status.
next_checks: Inspect generated `.puma` files and CIM-MLC `output/*_inst.txt` files before naming stable instruction or meta-operator fields.
Compare where each stack stores placement state: explicit generated code, reports, ONNX attributes, Python configs, or simulator configuration.
Check whether either artifact provides enough examples for a future detail-page matrix of source graph, placement object, backend stream, and simulator contract.

working_group_investigations.label: Public full-stack artifact boundary comparison
working_group_investigations.scope: Treat as a cross-cutting comparison of public or near-public stack boundaries where compiler state survives as JSON, YAML, assembly, meta-operators, simulator inputs, or generated scripts.
working_group_investigations.why_coherent: The common object is not a shared technology or group. It is representational visibility: a graph, mapping, resource assignment, instruction stream, calculation parameter set, or simulator contract becomes inspectable outside one monolithic backend.
continuity_evidence: CIMFlow exposes a vertical digital-SRAM-CIM path through ONNX input, hardware/config data, graph partitioning, OP-level MLIR/DSL lowering, per-core ISA JSON, synchronization IDs, and SystemC-backed simulation.

PUMA exposes a PUMA-specific backend contract through compiler-built model graphs, hardware mapping, per-core/per-tile assembly, XbarIn/XbarOut register classes, FIFO and shared-memory synchronization, and PUMAsim traces.

OpenCIMTC exposes a YAML CIM-IR split into Attribute, Mapping, and Calculation regions, carrying layer attributes, physical array placement, ADC/integration-time and quantization parameters, and generated simulator or hardware-oriented scripts.

CIM-MLC is supporting evidence because its semantics are distributed across ONNX annotations, architecture Python configs, tiered scheduling passes, and Core/Crossbar/Wordline meta-operator flows rather than a single stable dialect.
working_group_investigations.boundary: This comparison should not be read as one artifact lineage, and it should not absorb macro-only generators or benchmark-only runtimes unless they expose a similarly inspectable compiler/backend boundary.
next_checks: Inspect representative generated files from CIMFlow, PUMA, OpenCIMTC, and CIM-MLC before asserting compatible schema fields.
Build a small comparison table only after checking artifact examples: source graph object, mapping object, backend object, simulator contract, and mutable parameter surface.
Keep this as an inspection path unless enough checked examples justify a separate full-stack-boundaries detail page.
